meta.title: Consumer GPU VRAM Price Reversion by 2028
meta.question: By 2028-12-31, has the street price of top-tier consumer discrete GPUs (RTX 5080-class or nearest equivalent-tier) reverted to within 20% of the inflation-adjusted equivalent-tier consumer GPU price from 2024-Q4, as tracked by major GPU pricing databases?
meta.resolution: Resolved H1 (full reversion) if NowInStock, TechPowerUp, or equivalent shows RTX 5080-class 90-day average street price within 20% of inflation-adjusted 2024-Q4 RTX 4080 baseline (est. $1,050-$1,150 CPI-adjusted) for ≥3 consecutive months before 2028-12-31. H2 if 20-50% above baseline at resolution date. H3 if 50-100% above. H4 if >100% above baseline or NVIDIA exits consumer discrete GPU segment. resolutionDate: 2028-12-31.

tagConfig._docs: SUPPLY/HARDWARE favor H1/H2 (normalization). DEMAND/EARNINGS (hyperscaler) favor H3/H4. REGULATORY neutral.

H1.label: Full reversion: street price within 20% of inflation-adj pre-surge (Q4-24) baseline
H1.description: Malone's 'short-term market problem' thesis — new fab capacity (TSMC 2nm, HBM4), AMD competition, and inference efficiency gains normalize consumer GPU supply/demand. RTX 5080-class returns to historical tier pricing by 2028.

H2.label: Moderate elevated plateau: 20-50% above inflation-adj pre-surge baseline
H2.description: Data-center demand partially eases but doesn't fully normalize. New fab capacity reduces scarcity but doesn't eliminate it. Pricing settles above historical norms but consumers can still buy at some premium.

H3.label: Sustained high elevation: 50-100% above inflation-adj pre-surge baseline
H3.description: Alex's thesis — AI/data-center demand structurally outpaces consumer GPU supply. HBM allocation remains prioritized for enterprise. Consumers face persistent scarcity and elevated prices through 2028. lowrhoufo '10-year horizon' view plausible.

H4.label: Structural escalation/bifurcation: street price over 100% above pre-surge baseline or NVIDIA exits consumer GPU
H4.description: NVIDIA formally de-prioritizes or exits consumer discrete GPU segment to maximize data-center margins. Consumer cards become niche/legacy. Gaming GPU market bifurcates from AI silicon. Street prices extreme or consumer tier disappears as a market.

posteriorHistory.note: Initial priors. Sourced from Bsky thread (proptermalone/lastnpcalex, 2026-04-22) — moat/VRAM debate. H1 (full reversion) = 35%: historical pattern of GPU price normalization after demand shocks (crypto GPU crunch 2021-2022 normalized by 2023), HBM4 fab buildout expected 2026-2027, AMD competitive pressure. H2 (moderate plateau) = 30%: data-center demand may ease partially but AI inference growth sustains some premium. H3 (sustained high) = 22%: Alex's thesis — structural demand outpaces fab expansion; prior reduced vs. H1/H2 because historical precedent favors normalization. H4 (structural escalation/bifurcation) = 13%: NVIDIA consumer exit is possible but faces antitrust and brand risk; treated as tail. No fired indicators at creation. Priors reflect base rates from prior GPU price cycles plus AI demand novelty discount.

Structural HBM supply constraint projected through 2028-2029 per SemiAnalysis. Hyperscaler memory capex 4x since 2023 indicates sustained demand pressure. No pre-committed indicator fires (t1_hbm_supply_normalization requires consumer allocation RESTORED, not constrained). Directionally supports H3/H4. Likelihoods weight H3 highest given projected supply gap timing aligns with 2028 resolution.

redTeam.challenge: No significant shifts proposed; no challenge needed.

posteriorHistory.note: Current (April 2026) RTX 5080 spot price is ~25% above MSRP — within H2 band. t2_street_price_normalized (within 15% of MSRP for 3 months) NOT fired. anti_h2_extreme_escalation (5090 >200% MSRP for 1 month) NOT fired (5090 at ~91%). Not recycled_intel vs ev_003: ev_003 was Q1 2026 data with higher spreads (5090 at 75-200%); this is April 2026 snapshot showing partial softening on 5080 but persistent premium. H2 weighted highest since current 5080 price sits squarely in H2 band with 2.5yr runway.

redTeam.challenge: No significant shifts proposed; no challenge needed.

tier1_critical.desc: SK Hynix, Micron, and/or Samsung collectively announce HBM4 production volume sufficient for both data-center and consumer demand without consumer allocation cuts (confirmed in earnings or IDC memory report). Observable threshold: IDC or TechInsights reports consumer GPU HBM allocation restored to ≥2022 levels.

NVIDIA formally announces discontinuation or indefinite delay of consumer discrete GPU line (GeForce/RTX), dedicating all capacity to H-series/B-series data-center successors. Observable via official product roadmap announcement or 10-K filing.

tier2_strong.desc: 90-day average street price of RTX 5080-class (or current-gen equivalent) GPU within 15% of MSRP for ≥3 consecutive months, per TechPowerUp or NowInStock tracking.
AMD captures ≥25% of discrete GPU unit sales for ≥6 consecutive months per JonPeddie Research quarterly GPU report (increases competitive supply pressure).
≥2 major hyperscalers (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta) announce ≥20% GPU capex reduction in consecutive quarterly earnings calls — eases data-center demand pressure.

tier3_suggestive.desc: TSMC announces ≥30% YoY capacity increase for 3nm/2nm process node (increases overall wafer supply that feeds both GPU and HBM production).

Published research (peer-reviewed or major lab technical report) demonstrates >50% reduction in per-inference VRAM requirements for frontier-equivalent models — reduces per-query GPU demand and enterprise VRAM pressure.

anti_indicators.desc: Consumer GPU waitlists >4 weeks at MSRP persist into 2028-Q2 per NowInStock tracking — demand structurally exceeds supply, full reversion implausible.
RTX 5090-class (or equivalent top-tier consumer GPU) street price exceeds 200% of MSRP for ≥1 month — moderate plateau trajectory no longer credible.
RTX 5080-class GPU street price within 10% of MSRP for ≥6 consecutive months per TechPowerUp — sustained high elevation thesis falsified.
NVIDIA launches new consumer-specific GPU series with ≥xx70-class at ≤$800 MSRP before 2028 — confirms continued commitment to consumer discrete GPU market.

actorModel.description: Consumer VRAM pricing driven by NVIDIA supply strategy, AMD competitive pressure, HBM memory producers, and hyperscaler demand-side decisions.

nvidia.name: NVIDIA
nvidia.role: Supply-side monopolist — controls discrete GPU design and allocates HBM between consumer and data-center
nvidia.decisionStyle: Rational profit maximizer. Data-center gross margin (~70%) > consumer (~50%). Incentive to prioritize enterprise allocation.
biases: Short-term revenue maximization over long-term consumer brand loyalty
filters: Antitrust scrutiny if consumer GPU market is abandoned
overrides: If AMD gains >30% market share, NVIDIA may rebalance consumer allocation to defend share

amd.name: AMD (Radeon)
amd.role: Competitive challenger — benefits from NVIDIA supply constraints, incentivized to fill consumer gap
amd.decisionStyle: Opportunistic. RDNA architecture competitive at mid-range but trails NVIDIA at top tier.
biases: Historically slow to capture top-tier GPU segment despite competitive pricing
filters: TSMC capacity allocation constraints (shared with NVIDIA)
overrides: NVIDIA consumer exit would create a clear lane for AMD to capture enthusiast segment

hbm_producers.name: SK Hynix / Micron / Samsung
hbm_producers.role: Supply-side HBM memory oligopoly — allocation decisions between enterprise and consumer drive VRAM scarcity
hbm_producers.decisionStyle: Institutional/profit-driven. Enterprise HBM (H100/B200) at 5-8x premium over consumer GDDR. Rational to prioritize enterprise.
biases: Reluctance to invest in consumer HBM capacity when enterprise margins are higher
filters: Multi-year supply agreements with NVIDIA for data-center HBM
overrides: Consumer demand spike or government fab subsidies (CHIPS Act) could shift allocation

hyperscalers.name: Microsoft / Google / Meta / Amazon
hyperscalers.role: Demand-side — training and inference GPU capex directly competes with consumer GPU supply
hyperscalers.decisionStyle: Institutional/competitive. AI infrastructure seen as strategic moat; capex driven by competitive pressure rather than ROI.
biases: GPU hoarding behavior — buy ahead of demand to avoid supply constraints
filters: Quarterly earnings pressure if capex exceeds return thresholds
overrides: Macro downturn or AI investment write-downs could trigger rapid capex pullback

methodology: ACTIONS OVER RHETORIC — pricing trackers and earnings capex figures, not analyst predictions
WATCH HBM ALLOCATION — the single most important supply-side signal is memory producer allocation between enterprise and consumer
TRACK AMD SHARE — JonPeddie Research quarterly GPU shipments are the cleanest competitive signal
HYPERSCALER CAPEX IS UPSTREAM — Microsoft/Google/Meta/Amazon GPU capex guidance moves before consumer prices react

evidenceLog.text: @lastnpcalex.agency posted on Bluesky: 'I think that misunderstands the mechanism though. AI corps GPU demand 2x'd VRAM prices for consumers in < 1 quarter.' Responding to @proptermalone's claim that AI corps lack moat. No data source cited for the 2x figure.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. Claim requires source verification before it can be treated as DATA. 5-check: phantom_precision flagged (specific '2x in <1 quarter' claim without cited SKU, retailer, or date range). Source trust: 0.50 (unknown_fallback). Upgrade to DATA when underlying source (e.g., TechPowerUp price history, NowInStock data) is provided.

evidenceLog.text: @proptermalone responded: 'yeah, that's a short term market problem.' @lowrhoufo.bsky.social replied: 'Depends a lot on what short-term means imo, but it passes the sniff test on like a 10 year horizon for me.'
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. Both posts are opinion on duration, not verifiable data. 5-check: rhetoric_as_evidence flagged for both. 10-year horizon comment is analyst intuition — no model or data cited. Source trust: 0.50 (unknown_fallback).

evidenceLog.text: GOVERNANCE WARNING on posterior update: quorum_failure. Reason: Restore initial priors — corrupted during indicator patch (stamp_deadlines side effect). Correct values from posteriorHistory[0].

Multiple sources confirm consumer GPU prices elevated significantly vs. MSRP in Q1 2026, driven by AI/data-center demand crowding out GDDR7 and HBM supply. RTX 5090 ($1,999 MSRP) trading at $3,000-$6,000 street (75-200% above MSRP); RTX 5080 ($999 MSRP) at $1,599+ retail (45-60% above MSRP). RTX 5090 Founders Edition sold out in 8 minutes at launch. Server ECC RAM prices roughly doubled since Q3 2025. AI projected to consume ~20% of total DRAM production in 2026. 30-40% reduction in consumer GPU production allocation reported. Sources: TechSpot Q1 2026 GPU Pricing Update; BuySellRam memory economics; WccFTech RTX 5090 price trajectory; TrackaLacker RTX 5090 tracker; XDA Developers.
evidenceLog.provenance: TechSpot Q1 2026 GPU Pricing Update; BuySellRam memory economics; WccFTech RTX 5090 price trajectory; TrackaLacker RTX 5090 tracker; XDA Developers
evidenceLog.source: Multi-source corroboration (TechSpot / BuySellRam / WccFTech / TrackaLacker / XDA)
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. Verified DATA — prices elevated 45-200% above MSRP. Directional toward H3. No pre-committed indicator threshold met: t2_street_price_normalized requires WITHIN 15% (opposite); anti_h1_persistent_waitlist requires 2028-Q2 persistence (too early); t1_hbm_supply_normalization not yet fired. FLAG: add tier3 indicator for sustained >40% premium in 2026 at next design review.

evidenceLog.text: SemiAnalysis (via Tom's Hardware, 2026-04): Memory will consume ~30% of hyperscaler AI data center spending in CY2026, a 4x increase over 2023 (~8%). HBM demand growing 80-100%/yr vs supply growing 50-60%/yr; gap not expected to close until 2028-2029. HBM3E and HBM4 supply fully allocated through 2026 with hyperscalers locking multi-year contracts. NVIDIA retains preferential supply terms; AMD structurally more exposed to memory cost inflation.

GOVERNANCE WARNING on bayesian_update: confidence_inflation, quorum_failure. Reason: Structural HBM supply constraint projected through 2028-2029 per SemiAnalysis. Hyperscaler memory capex 4x since 2023 indicates sustained demand pressure. No pre-committed indicator fires (t1_hbm_supply_normalization requires consumer allocation RESTORED, not constrained). Directionally supports H3/H4. Likelihoods weight H3 highest given projected supply gap timing aligns with 2028 resolution.

April 2026 street price snapshot: RTX 5080 at $1,249 on Amazon (~25% above $999 MSRP); RTX 5090 at $3,818 on Amazon (~91% above $1,999 MSRP); cheapest AIB 5090 (ASUS TUF) at $2,909 (~46% above MSRP). Per BestValueGPU / TrackaLacker / Tom's Hardware trackers. RTX 5080 premium in H2 band (20-50%); RTX 5090 premium straddling H2/H3 boundary. 90-day rolling average not yet established.
evidenceLog.source: Tom's Hardware GPU price tracker / BestValueGPU / TrackaLacker

evidenceLog.text: GOVERNANCE WARNING on bayesian_update: quorum_failure. Reason: Current (April 2026) RTX 5080 spot price is ~25% above MSRP — within H2 band. t2_street_price_normalized (within 15% of MSRP for 3 months) NOT fired. anti_h2_extreme_escalation (5090 >200% MSRP for 1 month) NOT fired (5090 at ~91%). Not recycled_intel vs ev_003: ev_003 was Q1 2026 data with higher spreads (5090 at 75-200%); this is April 2026 snapshot showing partial softening on 5080 but persistent premium. H2 weighted highest since current 5080 price sits squarely in H2 band with 2.5yr runway.

rtx_5080_street_msrp_ratio.label: RTX 5080-class GPU 90-day avg street/MSRP ratio
rtx_5080_street_msrp_ratio.note: MSRP anchor: RTX 5080 = $999, RTX 5090 = $1,999 (NVIDIA launch pricing Jan 2025). Establish 90-day baseline on topic activation.

consumer_gpu_stock_availability.label: % of major retailers with RTX 5080-class in stock at MSRP

hbm_enterprise_consumer_split.label: HBM memory production split: enterprise vs. consumer allocation
hbm_enterprise_consumer_split.source: IDC Memory Tracker, TechInsights, SK Hynix/Micron earnings calls
hbm_enterprise_consumer_split.note: Proxy for structural demand pressure. Enterprise allocation >80% = structural constraint for consumer.

amd_discrete_gpu_market_share.label: AMD discrete GPU unit market share
amd_discrete_gpu_market_share.source: JonPeddie Research quarterly GPU shipments report

watchpoints: TechPowerUp/NowInStock: RTX 5080/5090 street vs. MSRP weekly
NVIDIA quarterly earnings: data-center vs. gaming revenue split (signals allocation priority)
SK Hynix/Micron/Samsung earnings: HBM production volume and customer allocation commentary
JonPeddie Research quarterly GPU shipments (AMD market share signal)
Hyperscaler (MSFT/GOOG/META/AMZN) GPU capex guidance in quarterly earnings
TSMC capacity utilization reports (3nm/2nm node — upstream of both GPU and HBM supply)
searchQueries: HBM memory supply data center consumer allocation 2027

dependencies._docs: Upstream: calibration-ai-corporate-moat-2028. If top-3 moat persists (H1/H2), training demand remains high — supports H3/H4 elevated prices. If moat erodes (H3/H4), training demand drops — supports H1/H2 price reversion. Downstream: calibration-local-frontier-inference-2030 (VRAM normalization enables consumer hardware for local inference).

upstream.reason: Moat persistence (H1/H2) implies sustained hyperscaler training demand → structural GPU scarcity → supports VRAM H3/H4. Moat erosion (H3/H4) implies reduced training demand and inference efficiency gains → supports VRAM H1/H2 reversion. If moat posteriors shift >15pp, recalibrate VRAM priors.

issues: Stale dependency: calibration-ai-corporate-moat-2028.H2 assumed=0.35, actual=0.5383, drift=18.83%
Stale dependency: calibration-ai-corporate-moat-2028.H3 assumed=0.28, actual=0.0632, drift=21.68%

warnings: meta.resolution may not be objectively observable — does it describe something a third party could verify?

predictionScoring._docs: Calibration infrastructure. Snapshots are auto-created by engine on each posterior update. Record outcome when topic resolves, then compute_calibration_report() for Brier analysis.

resolved.entry_a_text: Multiple sources confirm consumer GPU prices elevated significantly vs. MSRP in Q1 2026, driven by AI/data-center demand crowding out GDDR7 and HBM supply. RTX 5090 ($1,999 MSRP) trading at $3,000-$6,000 street (75-200% above MSRP); RTX 5080 ($999 MSRP) at $1,599+ retail (45-60% above MSRP). RTX 5090 Founders Edition sold out in 8 minutes at launch. Sources: TechSpot Q1 2026 GPU Pricing Update; BuySellRam memory economics; WccFTech RTX 5090 price trajectory; TrackaLacker RTX 5090 tracker; XDA Developers.

HBM3E and HBM4 supply fully allocated through 2026 with hyperscalers locking multi-year contracts. NVIDIA retains preferential supply terms; AMD structurally more exposed to memory cost inflation.
